nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-flowing or still swamp habitats with murky, darkly-stained (tannin-rich) water; dense submerged and riparian vegetation. Type locality is a sago swamp in the Mimika District near Timika, Papua Province, at 45–60 m elevation. Closely associated with the Aikwa and Iweka River systems in south-central New Guinea.

decorPreferences: dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite) for spawning sites
floating plants (frogbit, salvinia) to diffuse surface light and reduce skittishness
driftwood and dried leaves for tannin release and natural feel
low or gentle flow — avoid strong currents

diet.notes: Predominantly microinvertivore in the wild; feeds on zooplankton, phytoplankton, small insects, and invertebrates. In captivity, live and frozen micro-foods are strongly preferred: baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, moina, and microworms. Will accept crushed high-quality flake and micro pellets as a staple but benefits greatly from regular live/frozen supplementation. Small mouth — food particles must be appropriately sized. Fry require infusoria or paramecium for the first 5 days before graduating to baby brine shrimp.

goodWith: other Pseudomugil species (similar size and parameters)
small peaceful tetras (Ember tetra, Green neon tetra)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina — adults safe; fry may be eaten)
avoidWith: faster or larger fish that outcompete for food
boisterous community fish (tiger barbs, larger danios)

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen; FishBase species image for Pseudomugil luminatus. Verify availability and license before use.

summary: The Red Neon Rainbowfish is a tiny (≤4 cm) pseudomugilid blue-eye from the sago swamps of West Papua, Indonesia, formally described in 2016. Males display a striking red-violet body with an iridescent neon-blue dorsal stripe and elongated filamentous fin rays. Best kept in species groups of 8+ in a well-planted, soft-water nano setup.
careNotes: Pseudomugil luminatus was formally described by Allen, Unmack & Hadiaty in 2016 (Fishes of Sahul 30(1): 950–961) from sago swamp habitat near Timika, Mimika District, Papua Province, Indonesia. Museum specimens had been collected since 1997 but were misidentified as P. paskai; the species circulated in the trade from around 2011 under the informal names 'Pseudomugil sp. Red Neon' and 'Pseudomugil sp. paskai Red Form' before the formal description. It is classified IUCN Endangered (EN, assessed December 2019), with a very restricted wild range centered on the Aikwa and Iweka River systems of south-central New Guinea. A biologically mature aquarium is essential before introducing this species — it is sensitive to poor water quality and sudden parameter swings. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 1–10) with low flow best replicates its blackwater swamp origin. Adding dried leaves (Indian almond, catappa) or driftwood to release tannins is beneficial. Keep in groups of at least 8–10 to distribute male display aggression and reduce individual stress; a species tank or nano biotope with similarly-sized soft-water fish is strongly recommended. Males are conspicuously coloured — intense red-violet body, neon-blue stripe along the dorsal midline, and filamentous extensions on the second dorsal spine and first pelvic fin ray — and engage in near-constant fin-flickering display. Females are smaller, paler, and shorter-finned. Sometimes confused with Forktail Blue-Eye (P. furcatus) and Gertrude's Blue-Eye (P. gertrudae, fw-fish-103); P. luminatus is distinguished by the vivid red coloration, shorter body (≤4 cm), and West Papuan lowland swamp origin. Feed primarily with small live and frozen foods (baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, microworms); crushed micro-pellets and flake accepted but live foods improve conditioning and coloration. The species has a short natural lifespan (1–2 years in the wild); captive specimens kept well may reach 3 years.
breedingNotes: Continuous, prolific egg-scatterer with no parental care. Well-conditioned males display intensely to females and spawning occurs daily when the group is in good condition. Eggs are deposited individually on fine-leaved plants, java moss, or spawning mops; each egg measures about 1 mm and has adhesive threads. Incubation takes approximately 10–14 days at 24–26°C. Fry hatch tiny and require microscopic live foods (paramecium, infusoria) for the first 5 days before accepting baby brine shrimp nauplii and microworms. Fry grow relatively slowly compared to killiefish congeners. Remove adults or provide very dense plant cover as eggs and fry will be eaten. Spawning frequency and egg viability improve at temperatures in the upper part of the range (24–26°C). Breeding difficulty is moderate due to the need for live foods, soft water conditioning, and careful fry nutrition.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudomugil sp. 'Red Neon'
primary.notes: Primary reference. Provided adult size (30–35 mm SL), temperature (18–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness (36–215 ppm ≈ 2–12 dGH), minimum tank footprint (45×30 cm, ~41 L), diet, breeding method, lifespan, native range (Timika, Mimika Regency, Papua), habitat (murky riparian swamp), and compatibility. Note: page covers the pre-description 'sp. Red Neon' trade form, which corresponds to P. luminatus following Allen et al. 2016. A dedicated P. luminatus page was not live as of access date.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudomugil luminatus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Family Pseudomugilidae, Order Atheriniformes), max size (1.9 cm SL per FishBase — likely underrepresented; hobbyist sources consistently report up to 4 cm TL), native range (Indonesia, Papua Province), IUCN Endangered status (EN, assessed December 2019), and description year (2016, Allen, Unmack & Hadiaty). Trophic level 3.1 consistent with microinvertivore diet.

additional.name: ANGFA — Pseudomugil luminatus (rainbowfish.angfa.org.au)
additional.notes: Provided type locality detail (sago swamp, Mimika District, near Timika; 4°40.669'S, 136°57.046'E), habitat description (clear, darkly stained slow-flowing swamps at 45–60 m elevation), wild water parameters (temp 26–28°C, pH 4.8–7.2, conductivity 7–126 µS/cm confirming very soft water), Aikwa/Iweka River system location, and species entry history in the hobby (circulated from ~2011 as 'Red Neon' before 2016 formal description).

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Red Neon Blue Eye Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Trade care summary. Confirmed adult size 3–3.8 cm TL, temperature 18–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 12–15 dKH (used as dGH approximation cross-check), minimum 10-gallon tank, omnivorous diet accepting dry foods with live/frozen supplementation. Noted species timidity and need for groups; compatibility with adult dwarf shrimp.

additional.name: Aquadiction — Red Neon Blue Eye Rainbowfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Independent care guide. Confirmed max size ~4 cm, temperature 17–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 12–15, soft water preference, lifespan up to 2 years, omnivore feeding on zooplankton/invertebrates supplemented with live foods, egg-deposition breeding with ~10-day incubation, fry requiring paramecium initially. Family listed as Melanotaeniidae (likely an error; Pseudomugilidae is correct per FishBase and original description).

fish.breedingNotes: Continuous egg-scatterer; deposits adhesive eggs daily in fine plants or spawning mops. Incubation ~10–14 days. Fry require infusoria/paramecium for first 5 days, then baby brine shrimp. Soft, warm water and live-food conditioning needed for consistent spawning.
